text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel in the right hand to carefully separate the outer ovarian cortex from the inner ovarian cyst, while the left hand holds a traumatic grasper to stabilize the tissue. A visible plane is being created between the two structures.
The surgeon attempts to remove the dense and tense ovarian cyst, which may rupture during the procedure. Suction and lavage are performed to clear any cyst contents from the surgical field.

The ruptured ovarian cyst is visible, and a 5mm suction device is introduced to remove the thick chocolate-colored material. Due to the viscosity of the material, irrigation is used to dilute it, facilitating easier suction. Occasionally, a 10mm suction device is employed for more effective removal.

The surgeon cleans the blocked suction device and resumes suctioning the thick chocolate material from the ruptured endometrioma. Copious lavage is applied to dilute and remove the material, with the patient's head positioned upwards to prevent fluid from moving to the upper abdomen, ensuring thorough cleaning of the cyst area.
The surgeon performs repeated suction and irrigation using at least three liters of saline to clear the surgical field. The cystectomy procedure is underway, focusing on removing the ovarian cyst.
The surgeon uses a traumatic grasper to hold the inner cystic layer of the thick and densely adhered endometrioma, facilitating the stripping of the cyst from the surrounding tissue.

During the cystectomy, the surgeon carefully separates the cyst from surrounding tissues, navigating along a defined plane to minimize bleeding. Coagulation is avoided at this stage to ensure precise dissection.

The surgeon uses a traumatic grasper in the left hand to firmly hold the cyst, facilitating its removal. The cyst is gradually stripped away, alternating between the left and right hands for holding and stripping. The cyst is now almost entirely removed, with only a small attachment remaining, which is being separated.

The uterine manipulator is used to achieve anteversion, revealing the right ovary adhered to the posterior wall of the uterus due to endometriosis. The surgeon prepares to separate the ovary to prevent potential extension of endometriosis to the rectum.
Blunt dissection is performed lateral to the uterosacral ligament to separate endometriotic adhesions, avoiding the use of monopolar cautery.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate adhesions from the posterior wall of the uterus, carefully detaching the adhered tissue.
The surgeon uses a monopolar spatula to perform fulguration on the remaining endometrial tissue, applying a coagulation current from a distance in spray mode to minimize the chance of recurrence.

The surgeon uses a spark wave from a distance to fulgurate the entire area affected by endometriosis. Following the fulguration, suction irrigation is performed, and the process is repeated two to three times.

The surgeon performs thorough suction irrigation and repeated lavage to cleanse the surgical area. Care is taken to avoid direct contact with tissue during fulguration, aiming to burn only superficial endometriotic spots. An Interceed adhesive barrier is used to prevent adhesion formation.

The surgeon spreads the interseed, a dehydrated regenerated cellulose adhesive barrier, across the entire cul-de-sac to cover the endometriotic area. The ovary is repositioned posteriorly to ensure it is situated between the interseed and the cul-de-sac, preventing adhesion formation.

The surgeon spreads the interseed barrier throughout the cul-de-sac, ensuring it covers the endometriotic area. Saline is applied over the interseed, and excess saline is carefully suctioned from the cul-de-sac without disturbing the interseed.

The surgeon drops the uterus and removes the uterine manipulator. An endobag, specifically a gloves endobag, is introduced to contain the excised tissue. Both edges of the endobag are held using a semi-traumatic grasper.

The surgeon places the mouth of the endobag into the cannula and carefully pulls the cannula out. Using the varus needle technique, the port closure is performed by creating a loop within a loop to pull the suture out, approximating the muscle and rectus together.
